Personality is "any characteristic pattern of behaviour, thought or emotional experience that exhibits relative consistency across time and situations".

- Genetic factors
- Social factors, interactions with other people
- Socialisation, taught how to act and behave
- Cultural factors, wider social beliefs, values and motives
- Situational, specific experiences of the person

Two thoughts

1. Idiographic approach. Person develops in line with how they see themselves
2. Nomothetic(law setting) approach, personality if fixed and

## Idiographic

Self-image the way you describe yourself. e.g. brother, data scientists, etc.

Or tall, shy, etc.

Self-esteem, to what extent you like and accept yourself

Ideal self, the person you would like to be.

## Nomothetic theories

Personality models

- Cattell's 16 personality factors
- NEO-FFI - big five
- Myers-Briggs Type Indicator(MBTI)
- Colour personality
- Belbin Team Role Inventory

Jung

- Introversion versus extroversion
- Sensing versus intuition
- Thinking versus feeling

## Cattell's 16 personality factors

1. Abstractedness: Imaginative versus practical.
2. Apprehension: Worried versus confident.
3. Dominance: Forceful versus submissive.
4. Emotional stability: Calm versus high-strung.
5. Liveliness: Spontaneous versus restrained.
6. Openness to change: Flexible versus attached to the familiar.
7. Perfectionism: Controlled versus undisciplined.
8. Privateness: Discreet versus open.
9. Reasoning: Abstract versus concrete.
10. Rule-consciousness: Conforming versus non-conforming.
11. Self-reliance: Self-sufficient versus dependent.
12. Sensitivity: Tender-hearted versus tough-minded.
13. Social boldness: Uninhibited versus shy.
14. Tension: Impatient versus relaxed.
15. Vigilance: Suspicious versus trusting.
16. Warmth: Outgoing versus reserved.

From <[https://app.qa.com/course/theme-9-understanding-self-ai-1698/cattells-16-personality-factors/?context_id=15255&context_resource=lp](https://app.qa.com/course/theme-9-understanding-self-ai-1698/cattells-16-personality-factors/?context_id=15255&context_resource=lp)>

## Neo personality inventory

1. Openness  
    This measures an individual’s tendency to embrace novelty and how imaginative they are.
2. Conscientiousness  
    This measures an individual’s dependability; how self-disciplined they can be; and how much they prefer planned behaviour over being spontaneous.
3. Extroversion  
    This measures a person’s tendency to seek stimulation from other people. The six facets that are tested are assertiveness, gregariousness, excitement, warmth, positive emotions, and activity-seeking.
4. Agreeableness  
    This measures an individual’s tendency to be cooperative and compassionate rather than antagonistic or suspicious. The six facets that are tested are altruism, straightforwardness, trust, compliance, modesty, and tender-mindedness.
5. Neuroticism  
    This measures how prone an individual is to psychological stress. It examines an individual’s tendency to maintain a level of emotional stability and control their impulses.

From <[https://app.qa.com/course/theme-9-understanding-self-ai-1698/neo-personality-inventory/?context_id=15255&context_resource=lp](https://app.qa.com/course/theme-9-understanding-self-ai-1698/neo-personality-inventory/?context_id=15255&context_resource=lp)>

## Myers-Briggs

1. ISTJ
2. ISFJ
3. INFJ
4. INTJ
5. ISTP
6. ISFP
7. INFP
8. INTP
9. ESTP
10. ESFP
11. ENFP
12. ENTP
13. ESTJ
14. ESFJ
15. ENFJ
16. ENTJ

If you want to learn more about the characteristic tendencies of each of these personality types, read this [website](https://www.myersbriggs.org/my-mbti-personality-type/the-16-mbti-personality-types/).

From <[https://app.qa.com/course/theme-9-understanding-self-ai-1698/myers-briggs/?context_id=15255&context_resource=lp](https://app.qa.com/course/theme-9-understanding-self-ai-1698/myers-briggs/?context_id=15255&context_resource=lp)>

## Colour personality theory

The four colour personality types are:

1. Red: Competitive, action-driven, fast-paced, ambitious, focused.
2. Blue: Thorough, knowledgeable, logical, organised, quiet.
3. Green: Thoughtful, warm, approachable, empathetic, calm.
4. Yellow: Talkative, positive, sociable, energetic, engaging.

From <[https://app.qa.com/course/theme-9-understanding-self-ai-1698/colour-personality-theory/?context_id=15255&context_resource=lp](https://app.qa.com/course/theme-9-understanding-self-ai-1698/colour-personality-theory/?context_id=15255&context_resource=lp)>

![Diagram of the colour personality model – Red (assertive and driven), Blue (logical and quiet), Green (thoughtful and approachable), and Yellow (energetic and sociable).](data:image/png;base64,)

## Belbin team role model

The nine Belbin roles that emerged from the research are:

1. Resource investigator (the social role)
2. Team worker (the social role)
3. Co-ordinator (the social role)
4. Plant (the thinking role)
5. Monitor-evaluator (the thinking role)
6. Specialist (the thinking role)
7. Shaper (the action or task role)
8. Implementer (the action or task role)
9. Completer-finisher (the action or task role)

From <[https://app.qa.com/course/theme-9-understanding-self-ai-1698/belbin-team-role-model/?context_id=15255&context_resource=lp](https://app.qa.com/course/theme-9-understanding-self-ai-1698/belbin-team-role-model/?context_id=15255&context_resource=lp)>

References

References are provided, as is the standard in academic writing, to show the provenance of the ideas expressed. You are not required to follow through and read every reference. 

- Allport, G. W. (1937) Personality: a psychological interpretation. Holt. [Personality: a psychological interpretation](https://psycnet.apa.org/record/1938-01964-000)
- Aritzeta, A., Stephen Swailes, S. and Barbara Senior (2007) ‘Belbin’s Team Role Model: Development, Validity and Applications for Team Building’, Journal of Management Studies, 44, 1, available at: [Belbin's Team Role Model: Development, Validity and Applications for Team Building* - Aritzeta - 2007 - Journal of Management Studies - Wiley Online Library](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/pdf/10.1111/j.1467-6486.2007.00666.x?casa_token=Kx3ukpiv_08AAAAA%3AAQNhUZDfaQF9sEMBDZUoQQP5QW_S-e6I5eJy_0_IHL2CiP7Xlfzs90QDcKtc9yFBFt0guKNOvNbjwDI), (accessed 04-10-2024) 
- Belbin (n.d.) ‘The Nine Belbin Team Roles’, Belbin, available at: [The Nine Belbin Team Roles](https://www.belbin.com/about/belbin-team-roles#:~:text=The%20nine%20Belbin%20Team%20Roles%20are%3A%20Resource%20Investigator%2C%20Teamworker%20and,the%20Action%20or%20Task%20roles\).) (accessed 04-10-2024) 
- Cattell, Raymond B. (1947) ‘Confirmation and clarification of primary personality factors’, Psychometrika, 12, 3, available at: [Confirmation and clarification of primary personality factors | Psychometrika (springer.com)](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/BF02289253), (accessed 04-10-2024) 
- Cherry, K. (2024) ‘Cattell's 16 Personality Factors’, Verywellmind, available at: [Cattell's 16 Personality Factors (verywellmind.com)](https://www.verywellmind.com/cattells-16-personality-factors-2795977) (accessed 04-10-2024) 
- McCrae, R. R.; Costa, P. T. (1983) ‘Joint factors in self-reports and ratings: Neuroticism, extroversion and openness to experience’, Personality and Individual Differences, 4, 3, available at: [PII: 0191-8869(83)90146, -0 (researchgate.net)](https://www.researchgate.net/profile/Paul-Costa/publication/223940035_Joint_factors_in_self-reports_and_ratings_Neuroticism_extraversion_and_openness_to_experience/links/5a159095a6fdcc314924d335/Joint-factors-in-self-reports-and-ratings-Neuroticism-extraversion-and-openness-to-experience.pdf) (accessed 04-10-2024) 
- McLeod S. (2008) ‘Self-Concept’, Common Lit, available at: [Self-Concept by Saul McLeod | CommonLit](https://www.commonlit.org/en/texts/self-concept) (accessed 03-10-2024) 
- Mellett, E. (2023) ‘The Neo Personality Inventory Test (2024 Guide)’, WikiJob, June 8, available at: [Neo Personality Inventory Test: What to Expect (wikijob.co.uk)](https://www.wikijob.co.uk/aptitude-tests/test-types/neo-personality-inventory-test) (accessed 04-10-2024) 
- The Colour Works Foundation (n.d.) ‘The Colour Model’, tcwfoundation, available at: The Colour Model - [The Colour Works Foundation (tcwfoundation.org.uk)](https://tcwfoundation.org.uk/the-colour-model/) (accessed 04-10-2024) 
- Zarate-Torres, R. and Correa, J.C. (2023) ‘How goo d is the Myers-Briggs Type Indicator for predicting leadership-related behaviours’, Frontiers in Psychology, 14, available at: [How good is the Myers-Briggs Type Indicator for predicting leadership-related behaviors? - PMC (nih.gov)](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC10017728/) (accessed 04-10-2024)
